title: std::string basics
instruction:
`#include <string>` gives you `std::string`.

Key methods:
- `.length()` or `.size()` — character count
- `.at(i)` — character at index (bounds-checked)
- `[i]` — character at index (no bounds check)
- `.empty()` — true if empty
- `.clear()` — erase content

Run this.
successMessage: Strings are dynamic — += appends and the string grows automatically. .at(i) throws out_of_range if i is too large; [i] doesn't check — use .at() when you're not sure.

title: substr, find, replace
instruction:
String operations:

- `.substr(start, length)` — extract a portion
- `.find(str)` — returns the index (or `string::npos` if not found)
- `.replace(start, length, str)` — replace a range
- `.erase(start, length)` — remove a range
successMessage: string::npos is the 'not found' sentinel — always check before using the position. substr(start, length) — note: second argument is LENGTH not end index (different from Java's substring!).

title: String comparison and case
instruction:
C++ strings compare lexicographically with `==`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`.

Unlike Java, `s1 == s2` correctly compares CONTENT (not addresses).

For case conversion, use `<algorithm>` with `transform`.
successMessage: C++ string == compares content — no need for .equals() like in Java. transform with ::toupper converts case. ::toupper takes a char and returns the uppercase version.

title: to_string and stoi — convert between types
instruction:
Convert between strings and numbers:

- `to_string(number)` — number → string
- `stoi(str)` — string → int (stod for double, stol for long)

These are your main tools for parsing input and building output.
successMessage: to_string() converts numbers to strings. stoi/stod convert back. stoi throws invalid_argument for non-numeric strings — wrap in try/catch for user input parsing.

title: stringstream — build and parse
successMessage: stringstream builds strings with mixed types cleanly. Parsing with >> extracts whitespace-separated tokens. This is the C++ equivalent of Python's str.split() for simple cases.

title: string_view — efficient read-only strings
instruction:
`string_view` (C++17) is a lightweight, non-owning reference to string data — no copy, no allocation:

```cpp
void process(string_view sv) {  // accepts string, literal, or char*
    // read-only access, no copy made
}
```

Use it for function parameters that only read the string.
successMessage: string_view accepts a string, string literal, or char* without copying. Zero-cost abstraction. Use it for any function parameter that only needs to read a string — a major C++ best practice.

title: Build a word analyser
instruction:
No starter code.

Given `string sentence = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"`:

1. Count words (count spaces + 1)
2. Count occurrences of the word "the"
3. Reverse the entire string
4. Replace every 'o' with '0' (zero)
5. Print all results
hint: Use find() in a loop for counting occurrences. Use std::reverse from <algorithm> for reversing. Use transform or a loop for replacing.

codeChecks.message: Use find() to count word occurrences.
Use reverse() to reverse the string.

successMessage: String manipulation using find, replace, and reverse. These are the bread and butter of text processing. Chapter 4 done!
